attributes.stem:
A 58-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a 24-hour history of diffuse abdominal pain. She has a history of alcohol use disorder and early liver cirrhosis on ultrasound of the abdomen 12 months ago. She has frequent presentations to the Emergency Department with abdominal pain and often discharges against medical advice. She drinks approximately 8-10 standard drinks (80-100g of alcohol) daily. She has no other significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, she appears uncomfortable. Her pulse rate is 95/min, blood pressure 125/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. Her abdomen is distended with shifting dullness consistent with ascites. There is widespread abdominal tenderness without guarding or rebound.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate first investigation?
options: Magnetic resonance imaging of the abdomen
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features concerning for spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) in the context of cirrhotic ascites. The presence of abdominal pain and tenderness in a patient with known liver disease and ascites should prompt consideration of SBP and diagnostic paracentesis is recommended in all patients with new-onset ascites, especially with a history of known liver cirrhosis.

Diagnostic paracentesis is the most appropriate first investigation as it provides immediate diagnostic information through direct sampling of ascitic fluid. The diagnosis of SBP is confirmed by an ascitic fluid neutrophil count greater than 250 cells/mm³. Additional analysis includes protein levels, albumin, culture, and glucose. The procedure is safe with a very low risk of complications, even in patients with coagulopathy.

Ultrasound of the abdomen would not diagnose SBP, though it may be useful to guide paracentesis if there is technical difficulty. While ultrasound can confirm ascites and evaluate liver architecture, it cannot provide information about ascitic fluid infection.

Abdominal X-ray has no role in diagnosing SBP. It may show ascites but provides no specific information about infection and would only delay appropriate management.

Computed tomography of the abdomen, while useful for evaluating other causes of acute abdominal pain such as perforation or obstruction, cannot diagnose SBP. It is unnecessary in this case where the clinical presentation strongly suggests SBP.

Magnetic resonance imaging of the abdomen is inappropriate as a first investigation. It is time-consuming, expensive, and would not provide any additional useful information for diagnosing SBP.

The most common organisms causing SBP are enteric gram-negative bacteria, particularly Escherichia coli and Klebsiella species. If SBP is confirmed, immediate empiric antibiotic therapy should be initiated with a third-generation cephalosporin.
learningPoints: Diagnostic paracentesis is the first-line investigation for suspected spontaneous bacterial peritonitis in patients with ascites.
Ascitic fluid analysis should include cell count, culture, protein and albumin levels.
Early diagnosis and treatment of spontaneous bacterial peritonitis is crucial as mortality rates are high if left untreated.
references: Skipworth RJ, Fearon KC. Acute abdomen: peritonitis. Surgery (Oxford). 2008 Mar 1;26(3):98-101. Available online at ; https://www.1spbgmu.ru/images/home/universitet/Struktura/Kafedry/Gospitalnoy_hirurgii_2/literatura_eng/Acute_abdomen_peritonitis.pdf

Aithal GP, Palaniyappan N, China L, Härmälä S, Macken L, Ryan JM, Wilkes EA, Moore K, Leithead JA, Hayes PC, O'Brien AJ, Verma S. Guidelines on the management of ascites in cirrhosis. Gut. 2021 Jan;70(1):9-29. doi: 10.1136/gutjnl-2020-321790. Epub 2020 Oct 16. PMID: 33067334; PMCID: PMC7788190. Available online at ; https://gut.bmj.com/content/70/1/9.long
